---
title: "Earleaf Greenbrier (Smilax auriculata)"
slug: "smilax-auriculata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622942000/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Earleaf Greenbrier"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Liliales"
tax_family: "Smilacaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Liliales", "Smilacaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622942000/medium.jpg" alt="Earleaf Greenbrier" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/sebento">
sebento</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342114074">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Liliales
-   **Family:** Smilacaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=127910)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, flatwoods, and coastal dunes.

## Natural history notes

A woody, climbing vine characterized by its tough, evergreen leaves with small ear-like lobes at the base and its thorny stems. In Florida, it is commonly found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, flatwoods, and coastal dunes. It grows year-round in warm conditions and produces dark berries that provide food for wildlife, while its dense growth offers cover in native plant communities.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/127910-Smilax-auriculata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/3157)
